Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty loose. I would try to set aside time on weekends to catch up on videos and readings, but it never really stuck as a solid habit. I mostly just did what I needed to do to keep up, nothing more. With five online courses, I spread myself thin and couldn't give any single course the attention it probably deserved. I'd listen to lectures while doing other things around the house, hoping something would stick.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. If there was an assignment coming up, I'd suddenly find time to study, but otherwise, I'd let days slip by without touching the course materials. I wanted to be more consistent, but life kept getting in the way. Work and other responsibilities made it hard to stick to any fixed schedule, so I just went with the flow.
Q3: Usually, it was an assignment deadline or a notification that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if anything new was posted or if I'd missed something important. I didn't really log in out of genuine curiosity or excitement—it was more about making sure I wasn't falling too far behind. The reminders from the system helped push me to at least open the platform.
Q4: For a while, checking the LMS became something I did every few days, almost automatically. I think it happened because I was worried about missing deadlines, so I made it a habit to at least glance at the dashboard. But it never felt natural or enjoyable—it was more like a chore I had to check off. Once the pressure eased up, I'd go back to logging in less frequently.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me because I learn better by listening. I could play them in the background while doing other things, and that helped me absorb information without having to sit and read for hours. The discussion posts occasionally helped clarify things when other students asked questions I also had. But honestly, I didn't engage deeply with most resources.
Q6: I often ignored the supplementary readings and extra materials. With limited time and energy, I had to prioritize, and those felt optional even if they weren't. The discussion forums I barely touched—I only posted once the whole course. I just didn't feel motivated to participate when I was already struggling to keep up with the basics.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or the exam. If I could see how it would help my grade, I'd make the effort. But if something seemed like extra content or just nice-to-have, I'd skip it. With low resources and five courses to juggle, I had to be practical about where I spent my limited energy.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones I'd do because I had to, even if I wasn't enthusiastic about them. There was always this mental calculation—if it's not graded, why bother? I know that's not the best attitude, but when you're stretched thin, you have to make choices. Optional stuff always ended up at the bottom of my list.
Q9: Yes, I did a lot of studying offline. I'd download the lecture videos and listen to them on my phone when I didn't have stable internet. I also took notes by hand sometimes, which helped me process information better than just staring at a screen. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS, just me and my notes or recordings.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes or replaying downloaded lectures, and none of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd spend an hour going over material I'd already accessed once, trying to understand it better. The LMS only knows when I click on something, not when I'm sitting with my notebook trying to make sense of it all.
Q11: Definitely. I'd access a video once, download it, and then watch it multiple times offline over the next few days. My internet isn't always reliable, so downloading was essential. The LMS would show I watched something for a few minutes, but in reality, I might have listened to that same lecture three or four times on my phone.
Q12: There were definitely moments when engaging became harder, especially when I was feeling down. This sadness would creep in, and I'd lose interest in everything, including the course. During those times, even logging in felt like too much effort. The CBT sessions helped a bit, but the low mood made it hard to care about academic work when I was just trying to get through the day.
Q13: Workload from my other courses and personal responsibilities definitely affected how I used the LMS. When I was overwhelmed, I'd do the bare minimum—just enough to not completely fail. Fatigue was a big factor too; after a long day, the last thing I wanted was to sit through another lecture. I'd tell myself I'd catch up later, but later rarely came.
Q14: Yes, many times. I'd have the video playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. I'd scroll through readings without really processing any of it. It was like going through the motions—my body was there, but my brain wasn't engaged. Those sessions felt wasted, but I'd count them as study time anyway just to feel like I was doing something.
Q15: I postponed assignments all the time, even when I knew they were important. Part of it was the low motivation—I just couldn't bring myself to start. The other part was feeling overwhelmed and not knowing where to begin. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with the same excuses. It's a cycle I couldn't seem to break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. Without a deadline, I'd let things slide indefinitely. When a due date was approaching, I'd suddenly find the energy to log in and submit something. It wasn't ideal—I'd rush through work and probably didn't do my best—but at least I submitted. The deadline pressure was stressful but necessary for me.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change my behavior much. By the time I got feedback on one assignment, I was already stressed about the next one. I'd read the comments briefly but didn't have the energy to really reflect on them or apply them to future work. It felt like I was always playing catch-up, so feedback became just another thing to glance at and move on from.
Q18: The course structure was pretty standard, but having five online courses at once made it hard to keep track of everything. I appreciated when modules were clearly laid out, but I often felt lost about what I should prioritize. The structure didn't really accommodate someone like me who was juggling so much. I needed more guidance on what was truly essential versus what was extra.
Q19: The video lectures being available for download made engagement easier because of my internet situation. But the amount of content was overwhelming—there was always more to watch, more to read. The discussion requirements felt forced and didn't add much value for me. Having clearer, shorter learning paths would have helped me stay on track instead of feeling buried.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat. I logged in a lot, watched videos, but my assignment completion wasn't great. Sometimes you're trying to understand the material but still can't produce quality work. Or you're going through personal stuff that saps your energy for actual assignments. High activity doesn't mean you're in a good place to actually perform academically.
Q21: That wasn't really my experience—my low activity matched my low performance. But I can see how someone could study efficiently offline and not need to be on the LMS constantly. If you have good notes and understand the material, you don't need to keep logging in. The LMS is just a tool, not the only way to learn.
Q22: A course log can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but it can't show whether you actually understood anything. It misses all the offline work, the mental processing, the struggles you're going through personally. It's like measuring someone's fitness by counting how many times they walked into a gym—it doesn't tell you what they actually did inside.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine effort to understand it. It's not just showing up or clicking through videos—it's being mentally present and curious. For me, true engagement was rare this semester. I was more in survival mode, just trying to pass rather than actually learning. That's not real engagement in my mind.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling emotionally, how the sadness affected my ability to focus and care. It wouldn't show the hours I spent with downloaded materials or the times I tried to study but couldn't concentrate. It also wouldn't show that I wanted to do better but felt stuck. The data tells a story, but it's missing all the context that makes sense of it.
